text: laparoscopic primary repair of perforated diverticulitis by Dr. Andrew Iraniha.

This is a fifty-two-year-old female who presented with two days history of severe left lower quadrant abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting and fever and found to have sigmoid diverticulitis with microperforation on her CT scan.
Despite conservative therapy with bowel rest and antibiotics, she continued to have increasing abdominal pain and tenderness.
During the diagnostic laparoscopy, the surgeon identifies sigmoid diverticulitis with an intraabdominal abscess, noting the absence of gross fecal contamination in the abdominal cavity.
The video shows the surgeon performing a primary repair of perforated diverticulitis, with visible suturing of the affected sigmoid colon and drainage of the intraabdominal abscess.

The laparoscopic inspection reveals significant sigmoid diverticulitis with visible pericolonic inflammatory adhesions, a small interloop abscess, and the presence of frank intraabdominal purulent fluid.
The surgeon drains the intra-abdominal purulent fluid and irrigates the abdominal cavity with an antibiotic solution, ensuring thorough cleansing of the surgical area.
The laparoscopic view reveals microperforation in the sigmoid colon due to diverticulitis, with surrounding tissue showing inflammation but no visible gross fecal contamination.
To eliminate the requirement for temporary colostomy for this patient, it was decided to repair the perforated diverticulitis primarily.
The surgeon places multiple intracorporeal sutures using a two-hole etabond technique to repair the perforated diverticulitis.

Even though a sigmoid colectomy with temporary colostomy is the standard of care in these emergency situations, these patients would require second operation to take down their colostomy and reconstruct their colon after a minimum of three months.
However, in this case, to prevent a temporary colostomy and improve the patient quality of life postoperatively, it was decided to repair the microperforation primarily and drain the abscess.
This patient tolerated the procedure very well and was discharged home on day number three after the operation with two antibiotics.
The surgeon performs a laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy, followed by a primary anastomosis to reconnect the colon segments.
This approach did eliminate the requirement for creation of a temporary colostomy and improved the patient's quality of life tremendously within that three months interval.
Obviously, this surgical option would not be suitable in most emergency cases when there's gross contamination or when patients are extremely sick and unstable.
However, in selected cases, it could be the alternative surgical option.
